Programming with Python

Basic of Python

Instructor: Bishal Rijal

Collection types in Python

  • List
  • Tuple
  • Set
  • Dictionary

    • List in Python¶

    A list is an ordered collection of elements accessible by index, lists are mutable¶

    In [28]:
    l = [1,2,3,4,4,5] #
    In [29]:
    l[0]
    Out[29]:
    1
    In [30]:
    l[-1] #negative indexing starts at -1 from the end and comes to the start of the list in decreasing order
    Out[30]:
    5
    In [31]:
    l[-2]
    Out[31]:
    4
    In [32]:
    len(l) #len is a function that returns the length of the list
    Out[32]:
    6
    In [33]:
    l[len(l)-1]
    Out[33]:
    5
    In [34]:
    l
    Out[34]:
    [1, 2, 3, 4, 4, 5]

    Iterating through lists¶

    In [35]:
    for i in range(len(l)):
    print(l[i])

    1
2
3
4
4
5
    In [36]:
    for number in l:
    print(number)

    1
2
3
4
4
5
    In [37]:
    l
    Out[37]:
    [1, 2, 3, 4, 4, 5]
    In [38]:
    for i in range(len(l)-1,-1,-1):
    print(l[i])

    5
4
4
3
2
1
    In [39]:
    l
    Out[39]:
    [1, 2, 3, 4, 4, 5]
    In [40]:
    l.pop() #removes the last element and returns it
    Out[40]:
    5
    In [41]:
    l
    Out[41]:
    [1, 2, 3, 4, 4]
    In [42]:
    l.remove(4) #removes the 1st occurence of the element given as the argument
    In [43]:
    l
    Out[43]:
    [1, 2, 3, 4]
    In [44]:
    del l[1] #deletes the element at a specific index
    In [45]:
    l
    Out[45]:
    [1, 3, 4]
    In [46]:
    l
    Out[46]:
    [1, 3, 4]
    In [47]:
    l.append(5) #adds an element to the end of the list
    In [48]:
    l
    Out[48]:
    [1, 3, 4, 5]
    In [49]:
    l.extend([2,3]) #adds all elements of the list given as the argument to the end of the origional list
    In [50]:
    l
    Out[50]:
    [1, 3, 4, 5, 2, 3]
    In [51]:
    l.append([2,3])
    In [52]:
    l
    Out[52]:
    [1, 3, 4, 5, 2, 3, [2, 3]]

    2D lists¶

    In [5]:
    mat = [[1,2,3],[4,5,6],[7,8,9]]
    In [6]:
    mat
    Out[6]:
    [[1, 2, 3], [4, 5, 6], [7, 8, 9]]
    In [7]:
    mat[0]
    Out[7]:
    [1, 2, 3]
    In [8]:
    mat[0][0]
    Out[8]:
    1
    In [9]:
    for i in range(len(mat)):
    for j in range(len(mat[i])):
        print(i,j,mat[i][j])

    0 0 1
0 1 2
0 2 3
1 0 4
1 1 5
1 2 6
2 0 7
2 1 8
2 2 9
    In [10]:
    mat
    Out[10]:
    [[1, 2, 3], [4, 5, 6], [7, 8, 9]]

    Calculating the sums of all the rows¶

    In [11]:
    mat
    Out[11]:
    [[1, 2, 3], [4, 5, 6], [7, 8, 9]]
    In [12]:
    sums = []
    In [16]:
    for i in range(len(mat)):
    sum_ = 0
    for j in range(len(mat[i])):
        sum_ += mat[i][j]
    sums.append(sum_)
sums
    Out[16]:
    [6, 15, 24, 6, 15, 24, 6, 15, 24]

    Collection data types - Tuples¶

    • Ordered collection of elements accessible by index
    • Share similar structure as lists but tuples are immutable
    In [18]:
    t = (1,2,3,4,)
t
    Out[18]:
    (1, 2, 3, 4)
    In [63]:
    t[0]
    Out[63]:
    1
    In [64]:
    t[1]
    Out[64]:
    2
    In [65]:
    t[-1]
    Out[65]:
    4
    In [66]:
    t[-3]
    Out[66]:
    2
    In [67]:
    for i in range(len(t)):
    print(t[i])

    1
2
3
4
    In [68]:
    for num in t:
    print(num)

    1
2
3
4
    In [69]:
    t.append(4) #tuples are immutable

    ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
AttributeError                            Traceback (most recent call last)
<ipython-input-69-db1a4e4ef7c7> in <module>
----> 1 t.append(4) #tuples are immutable

AttributeError: 'tuple' object has no attribute 'append'
    In [70]:
    t += (4,)
t
    Out[70]:
    (1, 2, 3, 4, 4)
    In [71]:
    t
    Out[71]:
    (1, 2, 3, 4, 4)
    In [72]:
    t *= 2
    In [73]:
    t
    Out[73]:
    (1, 2, 3, 4, 4, 1, 2, 3, 4, 4)
    In [74]:
    tt = ((1,2,3),[4,5]) #nested tuple/2D tuple
tt
    Out[74]:
    ((1, 2, 3), [4, 5])
    In [75]:
    for i in range(len(tt)):
    for j in range(len(tt[i])):
        print(tt[i][j])

    1
2
3
4
5

    Collection data types - Sets¶

    • Unordered collection of elements
    • Sets are mutable
    • Cannot contain duplicate entries
    In [76]:
    a = {'cat','dog','mouse','parrot'}
b = {'mouse','elephant','cat','lion'}
    In [77]:
    a
    Out[77]:
    {'cat', 'dog', 'mouse', 'parrot'}
    In [78]:
    b
    Out[78]:
    {'cat', 'elephant', 'lion', 'mouse'}
    In [79]:
    a.union(b)
    Out[79]:
    {'cat', 'dog', 'elephant', 'lion', 'mouse', 'parrot'}
    In [80]:
    a.intersection(b)
    Out[80]:
    {'cat', 'mouse'}
    In [81]:
    a.difference(b)
    Out[81]:
    {'dog', 'parrot'}
    In [82]:
    b.difference(a)
    Out[82]:
    {'elephant', 'lion'}
    In [83]:
    a.symmetric_difference(b)
    Out[83]:
    {'dog', 'elephant', 'lion', 'parrot'}
    In [84]:
    s = set("hello")
s
    Out[84]:
    {'e', 'h', 'l', 'o'}
    In [85]:
    s.add("i")
s
    Out[85]:
    {'e', 'h', 'i', 'l', 'o'}
    In [86]:
    s.add("o")
s
    Out[86]:
    {'e', 'h', 'i', 'l', 'o'}
    In [87]:
    s.remove("e")
s
    Out[87]:
    {'h', 'i', 'l', 'o'}

    Find out all the unique items of the list¶

    L = [2,33,2,1,1,5,6,6,0]

    In [88]:
    L = [2,33,2,1,1,5,6,6,0]
l = []
for each in L:
    if each not in l:
        l.append(each)
l
    Out[88]:
    [2, 33, 1, 5, 6, 0]
    In [89]:
    list(set(L))
    Out[89]:
    [0, 33, 2, 1, 5, 6]

    Collection data types - Dictionaries¶

    • Unordered collection of key-value pairs
    • Mutable
    • Keys must always be unique
    • Only immutable types can be used as dictionary keys
    • Values can be any type
    In [90]:
    d = {"a":1,"b":2,"c":3}
d
    Out[90]:
    {'a': 1, 'b': 2, 'c': 3}
    In [91]:
    d["a"]
    Out[91]:
    1
    In [92]:
    d["c"]
    Out[92]:
    3
    In [93]:
    d["d"] = 4 #adds a new key value pair
d
    Out[93]:
    {'a': 1, 'b': 2, 'c': 3, 'd': 4}
    In [94]:
    d["a"] = 11 #updates the value for the existing key
d
    Out[94]:
    {'a': 11, 'b': 2, 'c': 3, 'd': 4}
    In [95]:
    del d['d']
d
    Out[95]:
    {'a': 11, 'b': 2, 'c': 3}
    In [96]:
    for key,value in d.items():
    print(key,value)

    a 11
b 2
c 3
    In [97]:
    for key in d.keys():
    print(key)

    a
b
c
    In [98]:
    for value in d.values():
    print(value)

    11
2
3
    In [99]:
    d1 = {"a":[1,2,3,4],"b":[5,6,7,8]}
d1
    Out[99]:
    {'a': [1, 2, 3, 4], 'b': [5, 6, 7, 8]}
    In [100]:
    d2 = {"a":{"x":12,"y":13},"b":(5,6,7,8)}
d2
    Out[100]:
    {'a': {'x': 12, 'y': 13}, 'b': (5, 6, 7, 8)}
    In [101]:
    d2["a"]
    Out[101]:
    {'x': 12, 'y': 13}
    In [102]:
    d2["a"]["x"]
    Out[102]:
    12
    In [103]:
    d2["b"]
    Out[103]:
    (5, 6, 7, 8)
    In [104]:
    d2["b"][-1]
    Out[104]:
    8

    List Operations¶

    Slicing:¶

  • It is used to get substrings or sublists from a list.
  • In Python List, there are multiple ways to print the whole list with all the elements, but to print a specific range of elements from the list, we use the Slice operation.
  • Slice operation is performed on Lists with the use of a colon(:).

    Syntax: list-name[start:end]
    • In [10]:
    a=[1,2,3,4,5,6]
a
    Out[10]:
    [1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6]
    In [11]:
    a[:]
    Out[11]:
    [1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6]
    In [12]:
    a[2:4]
    Out[12]:
    [3, 4]
    In [13]:
    a[2:]
    Out[13]:
    [3, 4, 5, 6]
    In [14]:
    a[:2]
    Out[14]:
    [1, 2]
    In [15]:
    a[-2:]
    Out[15]:
    [5, 6]
    In [16]:
    a[:-2]
    Out[16]:
    [1, 2, 3, 4]
    In [17]:
    a[-4:-2]
    Out[17]:
    [3, 4]

    List Comprehension

  • Python's list comprehension feature is an easy approach to building lists.
  • By applying an expression to each item in an existing iterable (such as a list, tuple, or range) and optionally filtering the elements based on a condition, it enables you to construct a new list.
  • List construction with list comprehensions is more readable and effective than using conventional for loops.

    • Syntax: [expression for name in list]

    In [20]:
    new=[x for x in a]
new
    Out[20]:
    [1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6]
    In [22]:
    double=[2*x for x in a]
double
    Out[22]:
    [2, 4, 6, 8, 10, 12]
    In [23]:
    filter=[2*x for x in a if x>3]
filter
    Out[23]:
    [8, 10, 12]
    In [24]:
    list=[['a',1],['b',2],['c',3]]
list
    Out[24]:
    [['a', 1], ['b', 2], ['c', 3]]
    In [26]:
    li=[n*3 for (x,n) in list]
li
    Out[26]:
    [3, 6, 9]

    Expressions can also contain user-defined functions.¶

    In [28]:
    def add(x,y):
    return x+y
li=[[1,2],[3,4],[5,6]]
    In [29]:
    [add(a,b) for (a,b) in li]
    Out[29]:
    [3, 7, 11]

    Other Examples¶

    In [33]:
    newlist = [x for x in range(10) if x < 5]
newlist
    Out[33]:
    [0, 1, 2, 3, 4]
    In [36]:
    pl=['C','C++','C#','Python','COBOL']
pl
    Out[36]:
    ['C', 'C++', 'C#', 'Python', 'COBOL']
    In [37]:
    [x for x in pl if x!='C']
    Out[37]:
    ['C++', 'C#', 'Python', 'COBOL']
    In [38]:
    [x.lower() for x in pl]
    Out[38]:
    ['c', 'c++', 'c#', 'python', 'cobol']
    In [56]:
    word="Ram is a good boy and good student"
new=word.split()
new
    Out[56]:
    ['Ram', 'is', 'a', 'good', 'boy', 'and', 'good', 'student']
    In [59]:
    num=len([1 for x in new if x=='good'])
num
    Out[59]:
    2

    List Methods¶

    Dictionary¶

    Task¶

    In [65]:
    d={'a':1,'b':2}
d
    Out[65]:
    {'a': 1, 'b': 2}
    In [66]:
    d['c']=1
d
    Out[66]:
    {'a': 1, 'b': 2, 'c': 1}
    In [67]:
    d['a']
    Out[67]:
    1
    In [69]:
    d['a']=5
d
    Out[69]:
    {'a': 5, 'b': 2, 'c': 1}
    In [77]:
    'a' in d
    Out[77]:
    True
    In [78]:
    'a' in d.keys()
    Out[78]:
    True
    In [72]:
    d.get('a')
    Out[72]:
    5
    In [80]:
    4 in d.values()
    Out[80]:
    False
    In [73]:
    def checkkey(dic, key): 
    if key in dic.keys(): 
        print("Element is present") 
        print("value =", dic[key]) 
    else: 
        print("Not present") 
checkkey(d,'a')

    Element is present
value = 5
    In [75]:
    def checkkey(dic, value): 
    if value in dic.values(): 
        print("Value is present")
    else: 
        print("Not present") 
checkkey(d,5)

    Value is present

    Thank you ! Questions?¶
